words.etymology_note: Old English 'beallucas' (testicles); by the 1860s meant 'nonsense'; Sex Pistols 1977.
Old English 'ærs' — cognate with Dutch 'aars', German 'Arsch'.
From Medieval Latin 'Bulgarus' — referring to heretical Bulgarian sect; meaning shifted through centuries.
words.definition: From Medieval Latin 'Bulgarus' — referring to heretical Bulgarian sect

words.etymology_note: Likely from 'by our Lady'; disputed. Mild UK intensifier.
Contraction of 'God blind me!' — minced oath.
Dates to 14th C Middle English — probably onomatopoeic.
From 'knacker' — a horse slaughterer; slang for testicles since 19th C.
Minced oath — euphemism for 'Christ'.
Stand-in for a stronger s-word; established 20th C.
Euphemism for a stronger f-word; also confection since 19th C.
American euphemism for s-word; 19th C onwards.
Minced form of 'damn'; 18th C.
Originally 'husks of no value' → worthless → mild exclamation.
words.definition: Originally 'husks of no value' → worthless → mild exclamation.

words.etymology_note: From Yiddish 'feh'; popularised 1920s.
Mild exclamation of frustration.
Minced form of 'Christ' via 'cripes'.
Contraction of 'od rot' — mild minced oath.
From Dutch 'pappekak' — soft dung. Polite now, very rude in origin.
Originally 16th C for a frothy drink; meaning 'nonsense' by 17th C.
words.definition: Originally 16th C for a frothy drink

words.etymology_note: Disputed origin; possibly from Hiram Codd's bottle + 'wallop' (beer).
19th C slang for rubbish; origin disputed.
From violin bows being flimsy and trivial; 17th C.
words.definition: From violin bows being flimsy and trivial

words.etymology_note: Originally slop fed to pigs; figurative by 18th C.
1920s US slang; possibly from Russian/Yiddish.
Late 18th C, from 'twattle' (prattle).
From Buncombe County, NC — a congressman's 1820 tedious speech.
Mid-18th C; made famous by Dickens' Scrooge.
words.definition: Mid-18th C; made famous by Dickens' Scrooge.

words.etymology_note: Originally a theatrical device to 'trap claps' from the audience.
words.definition: Originally a theatrical device to 'trap claps' from the audience.

words.etymology_note: Scots, late 20th C; origin uncertain — possibly from 'numps' (fool).
1960s UK slang; disputed origin — possibly from 'Walter'.
Originally slang for penis (1940s); meaning 'fool' from 1980s sitcoms.
From 'twit' (to taunt); as 'fool' from 1920s RAF slang.
1980s UK slang from Jim Henson's Muppets; gentle insult.
words.definition: 1980s UK slang from Jim Henson's Muppets

words.etymology_note: 1920s, from 'nit' (nothing) + wit.
Cockney rhyming slang: Berkshire Hunt → c**t. Most users don't know origin.
words.definition: Cockney rhyming slang: Berkshire Hunt → c**t

words.etymology_note: Originally northern English for penis; meaning 'fool' from mid-20th C.
Contraction of 'God's wounds' — 16th C oath.
'God's hooks' — referring to nails of the crucifixion.
Euphemism for 'Oh God'; 17th C.
Minced form of 'Christ'; late 19th C.
Medieval: a knight's attendant → rogue by 16th C.
words.definition: Medieval: a knight's attendant → rogue by 16th C.

words.etymology_note: Old English 'cnafa' — boy; meaning 'rogue' by 13th C.
17th C alteration of 'rascal'.
Origin disputed; 16th C.
From Italian 'monta in banco' — one who climbs on a bench (to sell fake cures).
19th C US — a shrewd, unprincipled politician.
words.definition: 19th C US — a shrewd, unprincipled politician.

words.etymology_note: Hiberno-English rendering of 'idiot'.
Softened form of the f-word; popularised by Father Ted.
Scots for 'crazy person'; origin disputed.
Irish slang: 'gob' (mouth) + 'shite'; someone who talks nonsense.
From an Australian racehorse (1920s) that never won; then bird → fool.
words.definition: From an Australian racehorse (1920s) that never won

words.etymology_note: From the Aboriginal word for a noisy pink cockatoo.
words.definition: From the Aboriginal word for a noisy pink cockatoo.

words.etymology_note: AU/NZ for an unrefined person; popularised 1980s.
Strong insult in UK/AU; anatomical origin. Medium in AU, stronger in UK.
words.definition: Strong insult in UK/AU; anatomical origin

words.etymology_note: Battlestar Galactica (1978 / 2004) — invented profanity.
Red Dwarf (1988) — invented profanity.
Farscape (1999) — invented profanity.
Firefly (2002) — fictional minced 'goddamn'.
Derogatory use since 14th C.
Used of women since 17th C — gendered; review case by case.
words.definition: Used of women since 17th C — gendered

words.etymology_note: From Old French 'damner' → to condemn; softened to a general intensifier.
Southern US minced form of 'damn'; 18th C.
Minced form of 'hell'; late 19th C.
Minced form of 'Jesus'; popularised 1920s US.
Euphemism for 'Jesus Christ'; 1920s US.
Minced form of 'God'; 18th C.
Euphemism for 'God'; 18th C.
Clipped form of 'Jesus'; late 19th C US.
Middle English 'crappe' (chaff, residue); allegedly popularised alongside Thomas Crapper the plumber.
1940s US GI slang; back-formation or metathesis of 'curd'.
Early 20th C US; possibly from actress Eleanora Duse or Duesenberg cars.
1920s US; origin disputed — possibly from Irish surname.
From Bologna sausage; meaning 'nonsense' by 1920s US.
1920s US; coined by cartoonist Billy DeBeck as a minced euphemism.
words.definition: 1920s US; coined by cartoonist Billy DeBeck as a minced euphemism.

words.etymology_note: Late 19th C US; possibly from French 'bois de vache' — dried cow dung.
Ozark/Southern US minced form of 'God damn it'.
words.definition: Ozark/Southern US minced form of 'God damn it'.

words.etymology_note: 18th C US; blend of 'eternal' and 'damnation'.
19th C US euphemism for 'concern' / 'damn'.
Regional US — 'sakes alive', 'for Pete's sake'.
Mid-19th C US; minced form of 'Christ'.
1920s US sportscaster catchphrase (Harry Caray, Phil Rizzuto).
words.definition: 1920s US sportscaster catchphrase (Harry Caray, Phil Rizzuto).

words.etymology_note: 1920s US; later popularised by Captain Marvel comics.
words.definition: 1920s US; later popularised by Captain Marvel comics.

words.etymology_note: Early 20th C US; euphemism for 'holy smoke of incense'.
Early 20th C US; possibly anti-Catholic origin (Friday fish).
Naval origin — children born aboard ship near the guns.
17th C English, common in 19th C US; reduplication of 'fiddle'.
words.definition: 17th C English, common in 19th C US

words.etymology_note: 1960s US campus slang; origin uncertain.
1960s US college slang; likely blend of 'dwarf' and 'feeb'.
1960s US; originally slang for penis, now chiefly 'fool'.
words.definition: 1960s US; originally slang for penis, now chiefly 'fool'.

words.etymology_note: From Gullah for 'peanut'; as 'fool' chiefly US Southern 20th C.
Mid-19th C US; 'lump' + 'head'.
1940s US; popularised by Moe Howard of the Three Stooges.
words.definition: 1940s US; popularised by Moe Howard of the Three Stooges.

words.etymology_note: 1940s US; popularised by 'All in the Family' (1970s).
words.definition: 1940s US; popularised by 'All in the Family' (1970s).

words.etymology_note: From Yiddish 'shmok' — penis; Yiddish origin keeps it strong in-community.
words.notes: Yiddish origin; some speakers find stronger

words.etymology_note: From Yiddish 'pots' — penis; meaning 'fool' in US English.
From Yiddish 'klots' — block of wood; clumsy person.
Biblical hunter; inverted to 'fool' by Bugs Bunny's mocking use (1940s).
words.notes: HERO WORD — feature prominently in early puzzles. Bugs Bunny etymology is a showcase card.

words.etymology_note: Coined by Jonathan Swift (Gulliver's Travels, 1726); US sense: boorish person.
Yiddish-origin for 'nothing of value'; literally 'goat droppings'.
Euphemism for 'Jesus'; popularised by Disney's Pinocchio (1940).
Modern US Christian-friendly euphemism for 'Jesus Christ'.
words.definition: Modern US Christian-friendly euphemism for 'Jesus Christ'.

words.etymology_note: Irish-American; 'by Jesus'; usually in 'scared the bejesus out of'.
Southern US; intensifier of 'hell'.
Popularly (and incorrectly) attributed to Thomas Crapper, 19th C plumber; actually earlier.
words.definition: Popularly (and incorrectly) attributed to Thomas Crapper, 19th C plumber

words.etymology_note: 'Peanut gallery' = cheap seats for hecklers; vaudeville-era.
1950s US; a flop or a fool.
words.definition: 1950s US; a flop or a fool.

words.etymology_note: 1903 US baseball slang; popularised by Fred Merkle's 'boner' play.
19th C US; 'chuck' = lump of wood.
Late 19th C US; popularised by Archie Bunker ('All in the Family').
1960s US; mechanical and anatomical double meaning.
words.definition: 1960s US; mechanical and anatomical double meaning.

words.etymology_note: Old English 'ge-recenian' — to recount. Southern US retains 'I reckon' where other dialects lost it.
Mid-19th C US; 'askew, crooked, all wrong' — Southern and Appalachian.
1940s NYC children's slang — corruption of 'decalcomania' (transfer tattoos) meaning flimsy/fake.
19th C US; the 'Betsy' may be Betsy Ross, but nobody's sure.
words.definition: 19th C US; the 'Betsy' may be Betsy Ross, but nobody's sure.

words.etymology_note: Southern US exclamation; truncated religious plea.
Truncated 'I'll be damned'; Southern US.
Cowboy origin — to tie a hog's legs; 'stuck, restrained'.
words.definition: Cowboy origin — to tie a hog's legs

words.etymology_note: Early 19th C US frontier slang; 'to cheat or swindle'.
Mid-19th C US; nonsense, drivel.
From 'jack' (male) + 'ass' (donkey); insult use 17th C.
Midwestern 'oh, excuse me' — viral meme-famous from ~2015.
words.notes: Very on-brand for a puzzle category

words.etymology_note: Minnesotan/Norwegian-American expression of dismay or effort.
words.definition: Minnesotan/Norwegian-American expression of dismay or effort.

words.etymology_note: Minced form of 'for Christ's sake'; 1920s US.
'Pete' is a stand-in for 'Peter' / 'God'; 1910s US.
Rhyming intensifier of 'jeez'; mid-20th C US.
Upper Midwest tag phrase; 'Fargo' (1996) made it iconic.
New England intensifier ('wicked good'); regional since 19th C.
1950s US surfer/beatnik slang; 'crazy person'.
1920s US boxing slang — a mediocre fighter; origin disputed.
words.definition: 1920s US boxing slang — a mediocre fighter

words.etymology_note: WWII US military acronym: Situation Normal, All F***ed Up.
words.definition: WWII US military acronym: Situation Normal, All F***ed Up.

words.etymology_note: WWII US military acronym: F***ed Up Beyond All Recognition.
words.notes: Stronger than SNAFU — note in card
words.definition: WWII US military acronym: F***ed Up Beyond All Recognition.

words.etymology_note: US military acronym: Bend Over, Here It Comes Again.
words.definition: US military acronym: Bend Over, Here It Comes Again.

words.etymology_note: WWII US military acronym: Things Are Really F***ed Up.
words.definition: WWII US military acronym: Things Are Really F***ed Up.

words.etymology_note: US military/aviation slang for a chaotic situation.
words.definition: US military/aviation slang for a chaotic situation.

words.etymology_note: Minced form of stronger military term; 'a chaotic mess'.
words.definition: Minced form of stronger military term

words.etymology_note: NATO phonetic for C.F. — a 'cluster' of the military kind.
WWII cartoon by George Baker; 'a hapless loser'.
Modern US military slang for a non-combat soldier.
words.definition: Modern US military slang for a non-combat soldier.

words.etymology_note: 1980s US hip-hop; 'bad, inferior'.
1790s US counterfeit coins; 'fake, bad'.
1990s US slang; ugly or broken.
2010s US internet slang; 'bitter or resentful'. Earlier Navy slang too.
1960s US drug slang → 1990s hip-hop; 'acting irrationally'.
2010s US hip-hop → internet slang; 'startled, shocked'.
1980s US hip-hop; clipped 'disrespect'.
2000s US slang; 'shoddy, unreliable'.
Southern US — sweet on the surface, devastating underneath. The passive-aggressive masterpiece.
words.definition: Southern US — sweet on the surface, devastating underneath

words.etymology_note: From Italian 'poltrone' (lazy person, coward), entering English via French in the 16th century. Shakespeare used it in 'All's Well That Ends Well'.
Shakespearean adjective meaning petty or quarrelsome, used in compound insults like 'pribbling rogue'. Appears in 'Troilus and Cressida' (c. 1602).
A gudgeon is a small freshwater fish easily caught — hence a gullible person. Shakespeare used it in 'The Merchant of Venice': 'a gudgeon caught'.
words.definition: A gullible or easily deceived person.

words.etymology_note: From Latin 'cancer' (crab, ulcer). Shakespeare used 'canker' as an insult meaning a destructive worm or corrupting influence, notably in 'A Midsummer Night's Dream'.
Origin uncertain — possibly from Latin 'stuprum' (disgrace). Shakespeare used it over 20 times across his plays, most memorably Othello calling Desdemona one.
Originally 'Jack Napes', a nickname for William de la Pole, Duke of Suffolk (d. 1450), whose badge was a ball and chain like a tethered ape. Came to mean any cheeky upstart.
A puttock is a red kite or buzzard — birds considered greedy scavengers. Shakespeare used it in 'Cymbeline' to mean a knavish, greedy person.
words.definition: A greedy or knavish person (from the bird).

words.etymology_note: From Old English 'lutan' (to bow, stoop). By the 16th century it meant an awkward, clumsy person. Shakespeare used it as a verb in 'The Winter's Tale' meaning to treat with contempt.
From Italian 'coglione' (testicle), used as an insult meaning a despicable wretch. Shakespeare employed it in 'The Taming of the Shrew' and 'Henry V'.
Shakespeare coined this in 'Merry Wives of Windsor' by adding '-ster' to 'lewd'. Originally 'lewd' meant merely ignorant (from Old English 'laewede', a layperson).
An Elizabethan word for hedgehog. The Macbeth witches chant 'Thrice the brinded cat hath mew'd, thrice and once the hedge-pig whin'd' — associating the creature with dark magic.
words.definition: An Elizabethan word for hedgehog, used as an insult.

words.etymology_note: From 'clot' (lump) + 'pole' (head) — literally a lump-headed person. Shakespeare used 'clotpole' in 'Twelfth Night' and 'Cymbeline'.
From 'fusty' (musty, stale) + 'lugs' (ears or a heavy person). Used in 'Henry IV Part 2' — Falstaff is called a 'fustilugs'. Meant a fat, slovenly woman.
Originally the short erect tail of a hare or rabbit (from Middle English). Used as an insult meaning a contemptible person — literally calling someone a rabbit's backside.
words.definition: A contemptible person (literally a hare's tail).

words.etymology_note: Originally the dried dung clinging to a sheep's rear wool. In Australian slang by the 1960s it flipped — 'a bit of a dag' became affectionate, meaning an endearingly uncool person.
Originally 1850s slang for a pimp (from 'bludgeoner' — one who carries a bludgeon). By the 1940s in Australia it softened to mean a lazy person or scrounger. J.K. Rowling borrowed it for Quidditch.
Australian slang from the 1890s, literally a bag of rats — implying someone unpleasant or eccentric. Often used affectionately: 'you old ratbag'.
words.definition: A mildly unpleasant or eccentric person.

words.etymology_note: Australian slang from the 1970s, derived from Australian Rules football — a dropkick is the least skilful kick. Calling someone a dropkick implies they are useless or idiotic.
Australian abbreviation of 'mongrel', used since the 1940s to describe an annoying or contemptible person. Also used affectionately among friends: 'ya mong'.
words.definition: A contemptible or annoying person (short for mongrel).

words.etymology_note: Australian slang from the 1890s for an annoyingly puritanical person. Possibly from 'wow' (an exclamation of disapproval). The word was used in political cartoons attacking temperance campaigners.
Back-slang for 'boy' — i.e. 'boy' spelled backwards. First appeared in 1850s Britain, then migrated to Australia where 'yobbo' became the standard form for a loutish, uncultured young man.
words.definition: A rude, loutish, or uncultured person.

words.etymology_note: First recorded in Melbourne in 1868, possibly from an Irish policeman's pronunciation of 'larking'. Originally described young street toughs; now often used approvingly for a cheeky rebel.
words.definition: A mischievous or cheeky young person.

words.etymology_note: Originally 1810s US/nautical slang for a clumsy recruit or marine. Possibly from Dutch 'kloot' (lump). Travelled to Australia where it stuck as a term for an awkward, clumsy person.
Australian slang from the 1940s, probably shortened from 'ning-nong' (a fool). Used affectionately — 'don't be a nong' — with no real venom behind it.
Australian slang from the 1940s for a fool, possibly derived from 'dilly' (silly) or the herb itself (i.e. a vegetable, meaning dim). Used affectionately: 'you great dill'.
Cockney rhyming slang: 'cobbler's awls' = balls (testicles). Hence 'what a load of cobblers' means nonsense. The dropped rhyme makes it socially acceptable while remaining anatomically rude.
Origin disputed — possibly Polari (gay slang) acronym 'Not Available For F***ing', or from 'naff' meaning a nothing/nobody. Princess Anne famously told paparazzi to 'naff off' in 1982.
Cockney rhyming slang: 'Hampton Wick' = dick (penis). Hampton Wick is a real village near Kingston-upon-Thames. The slang drops the rhyming word to create a perfectly innocent-sounding euphemism.
Cockney rhyming slang: 'raspberry tart' = fart. Blowing a raspberry (the rude lip-buzzing noise) has been British playground currency since the 1890s.
words.definition: A rude noise made with the lips; a contemptuous gesture.

words.etymology_note: Cockney rhyming slang: 'Khyber Pass' = arse. The Khyber Pass is a real mountain pass between Afghanistan and Pakistan — the British Army knew it well, which is probably how the rhyme originated.
Cockney rhyming slang: 'Brahms and Liszt' = pissed (drunk). Two of the most dignified classical composers, forever immortalised as slang for being paralytic in a London pub.
words.definition: Drunk (Cockney rhyming slang: Brahms and Liszt).

words.etymology_note: Cockney rhyming slang: 'plates of meat' = feet. One of the oldest and most recognisable examples of Cockney rhyming slang, attested from the 1850s.
words.definition: Feet (Cockney rhyming slang: plates of meat).

words.etymology_note: Cockney rhyming slang: 'porky pies' = lies. 'Telling porkies' is now standard British English, understood far beyond London's East End.
From 'nut' meaning head (since the 1840s). 'Nutty' (crazy) and 'nutter' (a crazy person) are standard British slang. Cockney 'off your nut' means insane.
From 1900s American slang, possibly related to 'dip' (a foolish person, from pickpocket slang). In British use, thoroughly mild — 'she's a bit dippy' means charmingly scatterbrained.
From 'barm', the froth on fermenting beer — hence 'frothing' with madness. Attested since the 1530s. 'The Barmy Army' (England cricket fans) adopted the word proudly.
Shortened from 'caddie' or 'cadet' — originally a man who did odd jobs at Oxford University. By the 1830s it meant a man who behaved dishonourably, especially towards women.
Victorian slang for someone who overstepped social 'bounds' — a person of low breeding attempting to enter polite society. The word drips with class snobbery and dates to the 1880s.
words.definition: A person of low morals or breeding.

words.etymology_note: From 'rot' — a person who is morally rotten. Peak usage was Edwardian (1900s-1910s), the era of Bertie Wooster. 'What a rotter!' was about the strongest thing a gentleman would say.
From 'blight' — something that withers crops. Applied to a person from the 1820s, it softened over time. By WWI, 'poor blighter' was sympathetic rather than hostile.

Origin uncertain — possibly from Scalloway in the Shetland Islands (known for small ponies, hence 'small/worthless'). In post-Civil War US, 'scalawag' was a white Southerner who cooperated with Reconstruction.
Originally the 'black guard' — the kitchen servants who marched at the back of a royal procession with the pots and pans. By the 18th century it meant a scoundrel. Pronounced 'BLAG-ard'.
From Romani 'kova' (person/thing). Used in Victorian thieves' cant to mean a man — 'a rum cove' (an odd fellow). Dickens used it frequently in his novels.
Possibly from French 'esquiver' (to dodge, slink away). Since the 1900s in British English, a skiver is someone who avoids work. 'Skiving off' remains standard British slang for truancy.
words.definition: A person who avoids work or duty.

words.etymology_note: Originally a deformed calf thought to be caused by the moon's influence (16th century superstition). Shakespeare used it in 'The Tempest' for Caliban. By Victorian times it meant a dreamy fool.
A snipe is a bird that feeds in muddy shallows — a 'gutter snipe' was a child who scavenged in city gutters. Victorian class insult dating to the 1850s, dripping with snobbery.
words.definition: A street urchin or person of low social standing.

words.etymology_note: From Old Norse 'blathra' (to talk nonsense). In Scots, a 'blether' is both the act of chattering and the person doing it. 'Ach, away and blether' is a cheerful dismissal.
words.definition: To talk foolishly; also a person who talks too much.

words.etymology_note: Scots for scrotum — 'baw' (ball) + 'bag'. In 2015, a Scottish Twitter campaign named a major storm 'Hurricane Bawbag', which trended worldwide. The word captures Scottish irreverence perfectly.
Scots for an annoying small person — like a yapping dog. From the sound of a small dog's bark. Used in Scottish Parliament in 2004, causing pearl-clutching among non-Scots.
Scots and northern English dialect for a cuckoo — and by extension, a fool (cuckoos being considered stupid birds). 'Hunt the gowk' is the Scottish name for April Fool's Day.
words.definition: A fool or simpleton (from the cuckoo).

words.etymology_note: Scottish slang for someone who makes a fool of themselves — they're 'getting roasted'. Common in Glasgow and Edinburgh since the 1990s. 'Pure roaster' is the intensified form.
words.definition: A person who embarrasses themselves; a fool.

words.etymology_note: Irish English from the 1930s, meaning broken, ruined, or exhausted. Origin unknown — possibly from Hiberno-English dialect. Used on 'Father Ted' and in Irish media, cementing its place in the lexicon.
From Irish 'gaimbín' (usury). A 'gombeen man' was a rural moneylender who exploited famine-era desperation. Now used broadly for any petty, grasping cheat — especially in Irish politics.
words.definition: A petty cheat or dishonest dealer (Irish).

words.etymology_note: Irish slang with Gaelic roots — 'gabhal' (fork/crotch). Used as a mild insult in parts of Ireland meaning an annoying or foolish person. 'Would you stop being a gowl' is peak Limerick.
Glasgow slang for a rural Highlander, from Scots Gaelic. Used by Lowland Scots the way Londoners might say 'country bumpkin'. Can be affectionate or cutting depending on tone.
words.definition: A rural Highlander; a country bumpkin (Scottish).

words.etymology_note: From 'land' + 'lubber' (a clumsy person, from Old English). Sailors used it from the 1570s to mock anyone useless at sea. A 'lubber's hole' was an easy route up the mast that proper sailors avoided.
words.definition: An inexperienced sailor or person unfamiliar with the sea.

words.etymology_note: The bilge is the lowest part of a ship's hull where foul water collects — rats living there were the filthiest creatures aboard. As an insult, it means a despicable, low-down person.

The disease caused by vitamin C deficiency killed more sailors than combat. By the 16th century 'scurvy' became an adjective meaning worthless or contemptible — 'thou scurvy knave'. Shakespeare loved it.
A scupper is a drain hole in a ship's deck. 'Scuppered' means sunk or defeated — from deliberately opening the scuppers to flood a ship. 'Well, that's scuppered us' means 'we're done for'.
Barnacles cling to ships and slow them down — hence an insult for a clinging, parasitic person. Shakespeare used it in 'The Tempest'. 'Old barnacle' meant a person who'd overstayed their welcome.
A swab is a mop used to clean ship decks — the lowest job aboard. Calling someone a 'swab' was standard pirate-era contempt for an inferior, dating to the 1690s.
words.definition: A worthless person (from the deck-cleaning mop).

words.etymology_note: From Dutch 'kielhalen' — to drag a person under the keel of a ship as punishment. The barnacle-encrusted hull would flay skin off. Now used figuratively: 'the boss will keelhaul me for this'.
words.definition: To punish severely (from the naval punishment).

words.etymology_note: From Dutch 'vrijbuiter' (free plunderer) — the same root that gave us 'filibuster'. Originally meant a pirate or privateer in the 16th century. Now occasionally used for any unscrupulous opportunist.
From French 'boucanier' — one who smokes meat on a 'boucan' (wooden frame), a Tupi (Brazilian) technique. Caribbean hunters adopted it before turning to piracy; the word followed them to sea.
The bilge is the foul water that collects in the bottom of a ship — a putrid mix of seawater, sewage, and rot. 'Bilge' as an insult for nonsense has been standard since the 1850s.
From Old English 'wrecca' (exile, outcast). A wretch was originally someone banished from their homeland — pitiable rather than contemptible. By the 16th century it became a standard insult.
The nuclear option in playground insults since the 1970s. 'Poopyhead' is the first compound insult most English-speaking children construct — a milestone in linguistic development, technically.
words.definition: A silly or annoying person (childish insult).

words.etymology_note: From 'mean' + '-ie' diminutive suffix. Children's standard insult since the 1920s. The Yellow Submarine (1968) featured the 'Blue Meanies', cementing the word in pop culture.
From Old English 'stincan' (to smell). 'Stinker' has been an insult since the 17th century — originally quite strong, it migrated to playgrounds by the 1920s and is now wholly harmless.
words.definition: An unpleasant person or something of poor quality.

words.etymology_note: American alteration of 'bogey' or 'bogeyman'. By the 1890s it meant nasal mucus in US dialect. Calling someone a booger is a child's insult combining the gross-out factor with the bogeyman's menace.
words.definition: Nasal mucus; also a mild childish insult.

words.etymology_note: American playground compound insult from the 1970s. Mike Judge immortalised it in 'Beavis and Butt-Head' (1993), turning a childish insult into a pop-culture institution.
Baby-talk reduplication for faeces, attested from the 1940s in American English. 'In deep doo-doo' became a political catchphrase when used about George H.W. Bush in 1988.
From Middle Dutch or Middle Low German 'tatelen' (to babble). A 'tattletale' tells on other children — perhaps the gravest social crime in playground politics, punishable by exclusion from games.
words.definition: To inform on someone; a telltale.

words.etymology_note: Possibly shortened from 'innocent' or from the Spanish 'nino' (child). Used since the 1590s — Shakespeare has 'ninnies' in 'The Tempest'. 'Ninnyhammer' is the deluxe version.
From 'wiener' (Vienna sausage). As slang for penis since the 1900s, and as a mild insult for a timid or feeble person since the 1960s. SpongeBob's 'weenie hut' cemented its playground status.
As an insult for an eccentric or crazy person since the 1950s — probably from 'nutty as a fruitcake' (1910s). The fruitcake's reputation for indestructibility may also play a role.
From 'nut' (head, since the 1840s) + 'case' (a hopeless instance). Emerged in the 1950s as a more emphatic version of 'nutty'. 'Nutcase' is now mild enough for children's TV.

'A nice bit of crumpet' — British slang for an attractive person since the 1930s. The word itself dates to the 1690s for the griddle cake. 'Thinking cap' or 'crumpet' also meant head in 19th century slang.
words.definition: An attractive person (British slang); also the head.

words.etymology_note: 'Noodle' has meant a fool since the 1750s, predating its food meaning in English. Possibly from 'noddle' (head). 'Use your noodle' — meaning use your head — preserves both senses simultaneously.
words.definition: A fool; also slang for the head.

words.etymology_note: 'Going bananas' (crazy) dates to the 1960s — possibly from the effect of bananas on monkeys. Calling someone a banana means they are silly or foolish. Also a euphemistic exclamation: 'oh bananas!'
words.definition: A silly person; also a mild exclamation.

words.etymology_note: 'In a pickle' dates to Shakespeare's 'The Tempest' (1611). Calling a child 'a right little pickle' means they are mischievous. The original pickle was the brine, not the cucumber.
words.definition: A mischievous person, especially a child.

words.etymology_note: 'Silly sausage' is quintessential British affection-disguised-as-insult — used for children and adults who've done something daft. 'Not a sausage' (nothing) is Cockney rhyming slang from the 1930s.

Calling someone a turnip implies they have the intelligence of a root vegetable. 'Turnip' as an insult dates to the 16th century. In football, England manager Graham Taylor was dubbed 'Turnip' by tabloids after a 1992 defeat.
Toads have been associated with ugliness and poison since medieval times — witches supposedly used them in spells. As an insult since the 16th century, it means a repulsive or despicable person.
From Old English 'wesle'. Weasels have been symbols of sneakiness since antiquity — Aristotle called them 'mischievous'. 'Weasel words' (evasive language) was coined by Theodore Roosevelt in 1916.
Biblical origins — the serpent in Eden made snakes a symbol of treachery. 'Snake in the grass' comes from Virgil's Eclogues (37 BC). Taylor Swift's fans reclaimed the snake emoji in 2017.

From Middle English 'curre' — a mongrel dog. By the 14th century it meant a cowardly, worthless person. Shakespeare used it dozens of times. 'Cur' packs more contempt per letter than almost any English word.
words.definition: A cowardly or worthless person (from mongrel dog).

words.etymology_note: From Old English 'raett'. Rats have been associated with betrayal (they 'desert a sinking ship') and disease since the Black Death (1347). 'You dirty rat!' — often misattributed to James Cagney.
From Latin 'vipera'. The Bible's 'brood of vipers' (Matthew 3:7) established it as the strongest reptilian insult. Shakespeare used it in 'Richard II'. Implies venomous treachery.
Magpies are known for stealing shiny objects and chattering incessantly. As an insult since the 17th century, it means either a thief or a person who won't stop talking.

Origin uncertain — possibly from 'Duncan' or from 'dun' (grey-brown) + '-key' diminutive. As an insult meaning a stupid person since the 1840s. 'Donkey's years' (a long time) plays on 'donkey's ears' (which are long).
Originally the rag wrapped around a tramp's foot instead of a sock — hence the lowest of the low. Victorian in origin, it persists as a solid British insult meaning a despicable person.

Probably imitative — suggesting something trivial blown away like air. Used since the 1840s to mean nonsense. Boris Johnson popularised 'piffle-paffle' as a dismissal, adding a reduplicative flourish.
From Yiddish 'drek' (excrement), from German 'Dreck' (dirt, filth). Entered American English in the 1920s via New York. Sounds exactly like what it means — harsh, guttural, and contemptuous.
From obsolete 'gobbon' (a piece, morsel), from French 'gobet'. In modern British English it means miscellaneous bits and pieces, or the innards of something — 'hand me that gubbins'.
Tripe is stomach lining eaten as food — the word comes from Arabic 'therb' (entrails). As slang for nonsense since the 1890s: 'what utter tripe!' — equating someone's words with animal guts.
From Old English 'dreflian' (to drool or dribble). The leap from saliva to nonsense happened in medieval times — 'drivelling' meant both slobbering and talking rubbish. Both senses survive.
Probably imitative of the sound of expelled air (i.e. flatulence). 'Don't give me that guff' has meant 'don't talk rubbish' since the 1820s. In Scottish dialect, 'guff' still directly means a fart.
Toledo, Spain was a centre of medieval Christendom — 'Holy Toledo!' substitutes for 'Holy God!' Sports broadcaster Milo Hamilton popularised it calling baseball games in the 1960s.

'Good God!' softened to 'Good grief!' — attested from the 1900s. Charlie Brown made it his catchphrase in 'Peanuts' (1950 onwards), and it became one of the most recognisable exclamations in American English.
words.definition: An exclamation of surprise or dismay.

words.etymology_note: A euphemistic substitute for the other f-word, used since the 1910s. Begins with the same consonant cluster so it slots neatly into the same emphatic position: 'the flipping cheek of it!'
words.definition: Euphemistic intensifier (substitute for a stronger f-word).

words.etymology_note: A euphemism for 'bloody', used since the 1880s. Eliza Doolittle's 'not bloody likely!' in 'Pygmalion' (1913) scandalised London — 'blooming' was the version polite people quoted instead.
Originally meant 'struck by divine lightning' — 'blasted by God'. Used as a mild curse since the 17th century because it carries the weight of damnation without actually saying 'damned'.
A euphemism for 'bloody', used since the 1910s. 'Ruddy' means red-complexioned, so the semantic connection to 'bloody' (blood-red) is transparent — but just indirect enough for broadcast.
Contraction of 'God's truth!' — attested from the 1890s. Became associated with Australian English through cricket commentary and 1970s TV. Richie Benaud reportedly used it on air.
From 'slack' (loose, lazy). Used for draft dodgers in both World Wars. Richard Linklater's film 'Slacker' (1990) reclaimed it for Generation X as an identity rather than an insult.
words.definition: A person who avoids work or effort.

words.etymology_note: From 'it's more than my job's worth' — the cry of a petty bureaucrat who refuses to help. Coined by BBC's 'That's Life!' show in the 1970s, which awarded a weekly 'Jobsworth of the Week'.
words.definition: A petty official who follows rules rigidly.

words.etymology_note: From German 'Schurke' (scoundrel) or possibly from 'shark' (a parasite). During WWI, 'shirker' was a devastating accusation — white feathers were given to men suspected of avoiding service.
words.definition: A person who avoids work or duty.

words.etymology_note: One who 'lays about' — i.e. lies around doing nothing. British slang since the 1930s. Often used in tabloid headlines about benefit claimants, dripping with moral disapproval.
words.definition: A person who habitually does nothing.

words.etymology_note: From 'doss' (a bed in a cheap lodging house), from Latin 'dorsum' (back). A dosser was originally a homeless person sleeping rough; it shifted to mean any lazy person 'dossing about'.
From Old French 'mescreant' (unbeliever) — originally a Crusades-era term for a non-Christian. By the 14th century it broadened to mean any villain or wretch, regardless of faith.
From 'waste' + '-rel' (pejorative suffix, as in 'scoundrel'). A wastrel squanders money, time, or talent. Victorian novelists loved it for dissolute younger sons frittering away family fortunes.
From Old English 'ceorl' (a free peasant). As the feudal system hardened, 'churl' sank in meaning from 'commoner' to 'rude, ill-bred person'. Shakespeare used 'churlish' frequently.
From Old French 'cravante' (defeated, overwhelmed). In medieval trial by combat, crying 'craven' meant surrendering — a permanent mark of cowardice. It still carries that weight in literary English.
From Middle English 'dastarde' — probably 'dased' (dazed) + '-ard' (pejorative suffix). A dastard is specifically a coward who acts meanly. 'Dastardly and Muttley' (1969 cartoon) kept the word alive.

From Old French 'recreant' (surrendering, cowardly) — literally one who 'recries' or yields. A medieval legal term: a knight who yielded in trial by combat was declared 'recreant' and lost all rights.
words.definition: A coward or traitor who surrenders.

words.etymology_note: From Latin 'captivus' (captive) via Old French 'caitif'. A caitiff was originally a prisoner — the word degraded to mean a wretched coward. Chaucer and Shakespeare both used it extensively.
Origin uncertain — possibly from Latin 'rogare' (to beg). In 1572, the 'Vagabonds Act' legally defined 'rogues' as masterless men who could be whipped and branded. Now it's almost a compliment.

Possibly from 'hamfatter' — 19th century slang for a bad actor (from a minstrel show song 'The Ham-fat Man'). Alternatively from amateur ('ham' as shortened 'am'). Means an actor who overacts outrageously.
words.definition: An actor who overacts; also a show-off.

words.etymology_note: Theatre slang: to 'corpse' means to uncontrollably laugh on stage, breaking character. Origin: an actor playing a dead body (a corpse) who spoils the scene by visibly laughing. Dreaded by directors since the 1800s.
words.definition: To laugh on stage when one should not.

words.etymology_note: Originally literal — old stages were raked (tilted), so 'upstage' was further from the audience. An actor standing upstage forced others to turn away from the audience. Hence 'to upstage' means to steal attention.
words.definition: To steal attention from someone; to outshine.

words.etymology_note: Imitative of the sound of something falling flat. Theatre and film critics adopted 'flop' for a failed production by the 1890s. 'Flopping' originally meant collapsing like a dead fish.
words.definition: A total failure, especially in entertainment.

words.etymology_note: From 'hackney' — a horse for hire (from Hackney in London where horses were pastured). A 'hack writer' produces work for hire without artistry. Shortened to 'hack' by the 1700s.
words.definition: A mediocre or unoriginal writer or performer.

words.etymology_note: Theatre criticism term for an actor so over-the-top they seem to be eating the set. Dorothy Parker reportedly described an actor as 'running the gamut of emotions from A to B' — the chewer goes from A to Z.
In theatre, 'mugging' means making exaggerated facial expressions to get laughs — from 'mug' (face, since the 1700s, probably from drinking mugs shaped like faces). A separate meaning from 'robber'.
words.definition: To overact using exaggerated facial expressions.

words.etymology_note: From actors' habit of calling everyone 'love' or 'luvvie darling'. Private Eye magazine popularised it in the 1980s as a gently mocking term for pretentious thespians. Now standard British English.
Plural of 'twat' — from Old Norse. Robert Browning famously misused it in 'Pippa Passes' (1841), thinking it meant a nun's hat, after misreading a 17th-century poem.
Plural of 'prat' — originally 16th century slang for buttocks (a 'pratfall' is falling on your rear). Shifted to mean a fool by the 1960s. Now one of the most satisfying British insults.
words.definition: Fools or idiots (British, originally meant buttocks).

words.etymology_note: From Old English 'bratt' (cloak), possibly via Irish 'brat' (cloth/apron). A 'brat' was a beggar's child wrapped in rags. By the 1500s it meant any ill-mannered child. Charli XCX reclaimed it in 2024.

From Old English 'gripan' (to clutch, seize). 'Gripe' meant stomach pain by the 1600s — the metaphorical leap to 'complain' (as if in pain) happened in the 1930s. 'What's your gripe?' means 'what's your complaint?'
American English from the 1930s — 'in a snit' means in a state of agitation. Origin uncertain, possibly related to 'snot' (both begin with an irritable 'sn-' sound cluster).
words.definition: A state of agitation or irritation.

words.etymology_note: From Old English 'clod' (a lump of earth). Calling someone a clod compares their intelligence to a dirt clump. 'Clodhopper' (one who hops over clods while ploughing) added the element of rural clumsiness.

From Old Norse 'alfr' (elf). An 'oaf' was originally a changeling — a deformed child left by elves in place of a human baby. By the 1600s it just meant a stupid, clumsy person. The fairy-tale origin is completely forgotten.

From John Duns Scotus (1266-1308), a brilliant medieval philosopher. His followers ('Dunsmen') resisted new learning, so 'Dunce' became an insult for the stubbornly stupid — a spectacular posthumous insult.
words.definition: A stupid person (from the philosopher Duns Scotus).

words.etymology_note: As slang for a defective product since the 1900s — originally an unsatisfactory car ('lemon laws' followed). As an insult for a person, it implies someone sour, foolish, or generally disappointing.
British slang for the head, attested from the 1860s. Originally a large marble (a 'bonce' or 'bouncer'). 'Use your bonce' means 'use your head'. Endures in playground and football commentary.
Origin uncertain — possibly from Romani 'loke' (a man) or Dutch 'blok' (a fool). Used since the 1840s, it's now neutral British English for a man, but retains a faintly dismissive edge: 'some bloke'.
From 'toss off' — British slang for masturbation since the 1700s. A 'tosser' is therefore literally a masturbator, but in practice it's a general insult for a contemptible idiot. Enduringly popular.
Australian soldiers in WWI mangled French 'vin blanc' (white wine) into 'plonk'. It came to mean cheap, bad wine — and by extension, anything cheap and nasty. 'Don't drink that plonk!'
words.definition: Cheap wine; also something of poor quality.

words.etymology_note: From Italian 'bimbo' (baby boy). In 1920s American slang it meant a tough, stupid man. By the 1940s the gender flipped entirely — it came to mean an attractive but unintelligent woman. A remarkable semantic journey.
Possibly from a dialectal word for a green woodpecker (associated with rural life). Since the 1810s, a yokel is a naive, unsophisticated country person. The urban-rural sneer in one word.

Probably a blend of 'chunk' and 'lump' — first recorded in the 1880s. 'Off one's chump' meant crazy (chump = head). As an insult, a chump is someone easily fooled — 'a chump and his money are soon parted'.
words.definition: A foolish or easily deceived person.

words.etymology_note: From Spanish 'bobo' (stupid), from Latin 'balbus' (stammering). The bird 'booby' was named for its tameness (stupidity). A 'booby trap' was originally set for fools. The breast meaning came much later (1940s).
words.definition: A stupid or foolish person; a blunder.

words.etymology_note: From 'guiser' — a person in disguise or costume (as in 'mummers'). In London slang since the 1880s, a geezer is a man, often with a slightly dodgy implication. In American English it means an old man.
From Romani 'nak' (nose). A 'nark' was originally a police informer (one who 'noses around'). 'Narky' means irritable or bad-tempered. Both have been British slang since the 1840s.
words.definition: An informer; also an irritable person.

words.etymology_note: Origin uncertain — legend claims it derives from T.W. Earp, an Oxford undergraduate mocked by J.R.R. Tolkien in the 1910s. More likely just an expressive coinage. Means a silly, insignificant person.
From Latin 'plebs' (the common people of Rome, as opposed to patricians). In 2012, a UK cabinet minister called a police officer a 'pleb' — the resulting 'Plebgate' scandal cost him his job.

Origin uncertain — possibly from the decorative braid called 'gimp' (something twisted). As an insult since the 1920s meaning a feeble or contemptible person. 'Pulp Fiction' (1994) gave it additional, darker connotations.

British slang from WWII for a black-market dealer — flashily dressed, dodgy goods. Origin uncertain, possibly Romani. The spiv was a stock character of British post-war culture, immortalised by Arthur Daley in 'Minder'.
words.definition: A petty criminal or black-market dealer.

words.etymology_note: From Latin 'crassus' (thick, dense, fat). Marcus Licinius Crassus, the richest man in Rome, bore the name — fitting, as 'crass' now means showing no sensitivity or refinement. Thick in every sense.
From Dutch 'boer' (farmer) — the same word that named the South African Boers. In English since the 1500s, a boor is a rough, unmannered person. 'Boorish' behaviour is the enemy of polite society.

From Middle English 'knobbe' (a lump). As slang for penis since the 18th century (from the shape). 'Knobhead' — literally 'penis-head' — is a standard British insult that sounds funnier than it is rude.
words.definition: Penis (British slang); also a foolish person.

words.etymology_note: From Old English 'clott' (a lump). As an insult meaning a stupid person since the 1940s — you're being compared to a shapeless lump. Tony Hancock's radio show popularised it: 'you clot!'
Originally slang for a pimp (from 1870s, possibly from French 'pensionnaire'). In modern British English, 'ponce' usually means an effeminate or pretentious man — 'don't be such a ponce'.
words.definition: A pretentious or effeminate man; a pimp.

words.etymology_note: From Scots dialect 'ming' (to smell bad), possibly from Old English 'mengan' (to mix). 'Minger' exploded in 1990s British slang meaning an unattractive person. 'Minging' means disgusting.
